rows.v: Scoring three goals in a single match by one player — one of the game's signature individual achievements.
He completed his hat-trick in the 88th minute, and the home crowd roared as the ball boy fished it out for him to keep as a souvenir.
Borrowed from cricket, where a bowler taking three wickets with consecutive balls earned a new hat. Soccer adopted the term for three goals, though they needn't be consecutive.
Moving the ball past opponents with a series of close touches while keeping it under control at the feet.
He dribbled through three defenders in a phone-booth of space, the ball seemingly glued to his laces as bodies lunged and missed.
From the old English 'drib,' to let fall in drops or small movements — the same root as a basketball dribble, describing the ball moving in short hops.
A pass played into the open space behind the defense for a teammate to run onto, often springing a scoring chance.
She threaded a perfect through-ball between the center-backs, and the striker was suddenly clean through on goal with only the keeper to beat.
Descriptive — the ball is played 'through' the defensive line. The term grew with the tactical vocabulary of the modern passing game.
An acrobatic shot where a player kicks the ball backward over their own head while airborne, body parallel to the ground.
He met the cross with a bicycle kick, scissoring his legs in mid-air, and the ball flew into the top corner for goal of the season.
Named for the pedaling motion of the legs in the air. Known as 'chilena' in much of South America, where it was popularized in early-20th-century Chile.
Playing the ball through an opponent's legs and collecting it on the other side — one of the game's most humiliating skills.
The winger nutmegged the defender with a casual flick, and the bench erupted; nothing wounds a defender's pride quite like a meg.
British slang of uncertain origin; one popular story ties it to 19th-century nutmeg traders' trickery, though the link to the football move is unproven.
Striking the ball cleanly out of the air before it bounces — a difficult, spectacular way to shoot or clear.
The ball dropped over his shoulder and he volleyed it first-time, catching it so sweetly the net barely had time to ripple.
From 'volley' meaning a simultaneous discharge, via tennis, where it likewise means striking the ball before it bounces. Ultimately from French 'volée,' a flight.
A quick exchange of two passes between teammates to slip past a defender, also called a 'give-and-go' or 'wall pass.'
They played a slick one-two on the edge of the box, the defender left flat-footed as the ball came straight back into the gap.
Named for its two-pass rhythm. The alternative 'wall pass' captures the idea of using a teammate like a wall to bounce the ball off.
A pass played from a wide area into the penalty box, aiming to set up a header or shot on goal.
He whipped in a dangerous cross, and three attackers and two defenders all rose together — a moment of pure chaos in front of goal.
Descriptive — the ball is played 'across' the field from the flank toward the center, a staple of attacking play since the earliest days of the game.
Scoring two goals in a single match — one short of a hat-trick.
Her brace turned the game on its head; two goals in five first-half minutes silenced a hostile away crowd.
From the hunting term 'brace,' meaning a pair (of birds or game). Football borrowed it as an elegant word for a player's two goals.
A coordinated defensive move where the back line steps forward in unison to leave an attacker offside as the ball is played.
The back four sprang the offside trap perfectly, stepping up as one, and the striker's celebration died as the linesman's flag shot up.
Named for 'trapping' an attacker in an offside position. It became a defining tactic of organized defenses through the 20th century.
Aggressively closing down opponents to win the ball back high up the pitch rather than dropping deep to defend.
Their high pressing suffocated the opposition, swarming every defender who touched the ball until a panicked pass was finally intercepted.
From 'to press,' to apply pressure. 'Gegenpressing' — counter-pressing immediately after losing the ball — entered the global vocabulary via German coaches in the 2010s.
A match in which a team concedes no goals — credited especially to the goalkeeper and defense.
The keeper kept his fifth clean sheet in a row, and the defenders mobbed him; in their world, zero on the scoreboard is its own kind of goal.
From the idea of a scoresheet left 'clean' of any goals conceded — a metaphor for an unblemished defensive record.
A defensive action to dispossess an opponent of the ball using the feet, ranging from a clean poke to a sliding challenge.
She timed the sliding tackle to perfection, taking ball cleanly and turning defense into attack in a single sweep of her leg.

From the general sense of 'to tackle,' meaning to take hold of or grapple with — shared with rugby and American football, though the soccer version forbids using the hands or body to bring a player down.
Closely tracking and guarding an opponent to prevent them from receiving the ball or creating chances.
He was assigned to man-mark the playmaker and followed him everywhere, even to the corner flag, refusing to give him a single yard.
From 'to mark,' to keep watch on or note the position of. 'Zonal marking' versus 'man-marking' is one of defending's oldest tactical debates.
Kicking or heading the ball away from your own goal to safety, prioritizing distance over possession.
Under siege in the box, the defender hooked a desperate clearance into the stands — ugly, but exactly what the moment demanded.
Descriptive — the defender 'clears' the danger from around the goal. A 'clearance off the line' is the dramatic last-ditch version.
A defensive setup where a team sits deep near its own goal in compact lines, inviting pressure to hit on the counter.
Two goals up away from home, they dropped into a low block, ten men behind the ball daring the hosts to break them down.
Modern tactical terminology; the 'block' is the compact shape of defenders, and 'low' describes how deep toward their own goal they sit.
A kick awarded when the defending team puts the ball over its own goal line, taken from the corner arc — a prime scoring chance.
The corner curled in toward the near post, and their towering center-back ghosted in to thump a header past the stranded keeper.
Descriptive — the kick is taken from the corner of the field. It has been part of the game since the laws were codified in the 19th century.
A kick awarded after a foul, taken from where the offense occurred — either a direct shot on goal or a pass to a teammate.
From 25 yards he stood over the free-kick, took three steps, and bent it over the wall into the top corner — the keeper never moved.
Descriptive — the kick is taken 'free' of any opponent within the required distance. Direct and indirect free-kicks have been distinguished since the early laws.
A tiebreaker of alternating penalty kicks used to decide a drawn knockout match — football's most nerve-shredding finale.
It came down to the fifth kick of the shootout; he placed the ball, exhaled, and the weight of a nation pressed down on a single swing of his boot.
Introduced in the 1970s to replace coin tosses and replays for deciding tied matches, and now the dramatic climax of major tournaments.
Returning the ball to play by throwing it with both hands from behind the head, after it crosses the sideline.
His long throw-in was a weapon in itself, hurled into the box like a corner and causing the same panic among defenders.
One of the oldest restart laws, and notable as the only time an outfield player legally uses their hands — a quirk dating to the game's earliest rules.
A line of defending players standing shoulder to shoulder to block a free-kick's path to goal.
The wall jumped as one, but he'd drilled the free-kick low underneath them, exploiting the gap their own leap had opened.
Named for the literal human 'wall' formed in front of the kicker. Goalkeepers organize its position to cover one side while they guard the other.
Any restart of play from a dead-ball situation — corner, free-kick, throw-in, or penalty — that teams rehearse as planned routines.
They'd practiced the set-piece all week, a decoy run dragging a marker away before the real target arrived unmarked at the back post.
From the idea of a 'set' or staged, pre-arranged play, as opposed to open, flowing action. A large share of goals come from these rehearsed moments.
Video Assistant Referee — officials reviewing decisions on goals, penalties, red cards, and mistaken identity via replay.
The goal stood, then didn't; after a long VAR check the offside lines appeared and the celebration curdled into furious disbelief.
Introduced to top-level football in the late 2010s to correct clear and obvious errors, and instantly one of the sport's most divisive innovations.
A card shown for a serious foul or second bookable offense, sending the player off and forcing their team to continue a man down.
The reckless lunge earned a straight red, and his ten teammates traded glances — forty minutes to survive with a man fewer.
Part of the color-coded system introduced at the 1970 World Cup, reportedly inspired by traffic lights so decisions read clearly across language barriers.
A caution shown for a less serious offense; two in one match equal a red and an ejection.
Already on a yellow, he pulled out of a challenge he'd normally fly into — one rash moment now and he'd leave his team short.
The companion to the red in the 1970 color-coded system; the idea came to referee Ken Aston while sitting at a traffic light.
A player is offside if they are nearer the opponents' goal line than both the ball and the second-last defender when the ball is played to them.
He timed his run a half-second early and was caught offside by inches — the flag denying what would have been a stunning solo goal.
One of football's oldest and most debated laws, present since the 19th century to stop attackers from simply camping by the goal.
An offense in which a player other than the goalkeeper deliberately touches the ball with the hand or arm.
The defender's arm was raised and the ball struck it; penalty given, and a long argument began over whether it was truly deliberate.
As old as the game's separation from rugby, when handling the ball became the defining forbidden act for outfield players. Its exact interpretation is endlessly contested.
Extra minutes added at the end of each half to make up for time lost to injuries, substitutions, and delays. Also 'injury time.'
Deep into stoppage time, with the board showing six added minutes, he rose to head home the equalizer — the cruelest, sweetest of late goals.
Born from the referee's duty to account for 'time lost,' a power written into the laws so that wasted minutes don't simply vanish from the game.
When a referee lets play continue after a foul because stopping it would benefit the offending team — 'playing the advantage.'
The referee waved play on, advantage, and seconds later the attack he'd allowed to continue ended in the back of the net.
From the principle that the fouled team should not be penalized twice; the referee judges whether stopping play would hand the offender an unfair reprieve.
An affectionate nickname for soccer itself, evoking its flow, artistry, and global appeal.
On nights like this — a flowing team move finished with a flick — you understood exactly why they call it the beautiful game.
Popularized by Brazilian legend Pelé, who titled his autobiography with the phrase ('o jogo bonito'), though its use predates him in football writing.
Hardcore, organized supporter groups known for choreographed displays, banners, flares, and unwavering, often intense devotion.
The ultras unfurled a banner covering the entire stand, a wall of color and smoke, their drums never pausing for ninety minutes straight.
From the Italian 'ultrà,' meaning extreme, the movement took root in 1960s-70s Italy and spread across the football world.
The fixture between Real Madrid and FC Barcelona — one of the fiercest and most-watched club rivalries on earth.
El Clásico stops the country; for ninety minutes the political and sporting history of two cities is poured into a single football match.
Spanish for 'the classic,' the term marks the meeting of Spain's two giants, charged with regional, historical, and political rivalry far beyond sport.
A match between two local rival clubs from the same city or region, usually the most heated game on the calendar.
The local derby divided families and workplaces for a week; in this city you were one or the other, and everyone knew which.
Often traced to Derby, England, and possibly the chaotic 'Shrovetide' folk-football games there — though the exact origin of the sporting sense is debated.
Winning three major trophies in a single season — typically the domestic league, domestic cup, and the top continental competition.
They sealed the treble in June, an exhausted, jubilant squad lifting a third trophy that would etch the season into the club's history forever.
Descriptive, from 'treble' meaning threefold. The continental treble is one of the rarest and most celebrated achievements in the club game.
Playing ultra-defensively with nearly the whole team behind the ball, leaving almost no attacking intent.
Clinging to a one-goal lead, they parked the bus completely, eleven men camped in their own box daring anyone to find a way through.
A vivid modern idiom — as if a bus were parked across the goal — popularized in English football in the 2000s to describe stifling, defense-only tactics.
